text.title: Subscription Metrics and Billing Operations
text.summary: Essential subscription metric formulas (MRR, ARR, churn, LTV, NRR); billing best practices; dunning sequence for payment recovery.

rule.statement: Track Net Revenue Retention (NRR) as the single health metric. NRR above 100% means expansion revenue exceeds churn; below 100% means the business is leaking faster than it's filling.
rule.rationale: NRR captures both churn and expansion in one number. A business with 8% monthly churn but 12% expansion can grow despite the churn. NRR is the metric investors use to assess subscription business quality.

list.item: MRR = sum of all monthly subscriptions (excluding one-time fees).
ARR = MRR x 12 (for annual planning only; don't track day-to-day in ARR).
Monthly churn rate = customers lost / customers at start of period.
LTV = ARPU / monthly churn rate (higher ARPU and lower churn compound LTV significantly).
NRR = (Starting MRR + Expansion MRR - Churn MRR) / Starting MRR x 100.

rule.statement: Implement dunning automation before launch: retry failed payments on Day 1, 3, and 7 with accompanying emails; downgrade to free on Day 10. This recovers 20–40% of would-be churned revenue.
rule.rationale: 15–25% of subscription cancellations are involuntary (expired cards, insufficient funds). Without automated recovery, this becomes silent permanent churn. Most customers with failed payments want to keep the subscription.

example.note: 5-step payment recovery flow. Each step includes email + automatic retry attempt.
example.code: Day 0: Payment fails → log event Day 1: Email (friendly notice) + retry charge Day 3: Email (urgency) + retry charge Day 7: Email (final warning: access ends in 3 days) + retry Day 10: Downgrade to free tier, data retained 30 days Day 30: Win-back campaign email to churned users

antipattern.description: No annual billing option at launch.
antipattern.reason: Annual subscribers churn 3–5x less. Missing annual billing at launch means all early customers are monthly, with no commitment lock-in and no cash flow improvement.

antipattern.description: Making cancellation difficult (multi-step forms, mandatory calls).
antipattern.reason: Difficult cancellation increases chargebacks, generates negative reviews, and is illegal in some jurisdictions. Easy cancellation builds trust and sometimes produces "saved" customers who were testing how painful it is.
